title: Main feed endpoint /api/feed takes 4.15s, leaving the user on 'Loading stories...' for ~4 seconds
description: GET /api/feed?feed=all returns 200 but only after 4151ms (TTFB 4149ms: request issued at 602ms, response received at 4751ms). It is the data source for the homepage story feed, so until it resolves the main content area renders a 'Loading stories...' placeholder. Three sibling API calls invoked at the same instant (/api/communities, /api/daily, /api/featured) all returned in ~700ms, so this is latency specific to the feed handler's own work, not a platform-wide cold start. The response body shows the handler aggregates 'mixed' sources including live Hacker News content (story ids like hn-48510010 and summaries 'A discussion-driven story from Hacker News'), pointing to slow server-side external fetching/aggregation.
expected_behavior: The feed endpoint should respond in well under 1s (comparable to its sibling endpoints at ~700ms), so stories and their images render promptly instead of leaving the user staring at a loading placeholder for ~4s.
actual_behavior: The homepage feed request /api/feed?feed=all takes 4151ms to respond. The main content area shows 'Loading stories...' for roughly 4 seconds before any story appears, and the feed's story images cannot begin loading until the response arrives (second image batch starts at 4884ms).

chain.text: Because /api/feed does not respond until 4751ms, the feed query (TanStack Query) stays in its loading state and the homepage renders the 'Loading stories...' placeholder for ~4 seconds instead of story cards (confirmed by the screenshot at 1575ms).

root_cause.text: The /api/feed?feed=all handler's own server-side processing takes ~4.1s before it sends any bytes (TTFB 4149ms: request@602ms -> response@4751ms), and this is application-level work, not a serverless cold start. Proof it is not a cold start: three sibling Netlify functions (/api/communities, /api/daily, /api/featured) were invoked in the SAME 598-602ms window and each responded in ~700ms, so any cold-start/runtime-init overhead is bounded at ~700ms and already accounted for; the feed endpoint's extra ~3.4s over its siblings is work specific to the feed handler. That extra work is synchronous aggregation of externally-sourced content performed at request time: the response is source:'mixed' and demonstrably contains live Hacker News items (story ids such as hn-48510010 and summaries reading 'A discussion-driven story from Hacker News') merged with the local seed stories, content the server must fetch from an external source on each request. The 24KB (brotli-compressed) JSON body is modest, so transfer time is negligible and the 4149ms is server processing/wait time, not payload size. No backend (server-side) Replay recording is available for deeper profiling: the response carries no `replay-request-id` header (the identifier the backend-recorder workflow consumes) -- only Netlify's own `x-nf-request-id: 01KTZ28YYSE87Q7K0B0EYE2HVZ`, which is not a Replay backend-recorder request id and cannot be submitted to the recorder service, so client-side timing plus the response-payload composition is the complete evidence obtainable here.

judge_assessment.assessment:
Evidence: Screenshot 1 and 2 show the Digg homepage with 'Loading stories...' placeholder text in the main content area at t=1575ms, confirming the loading state. Screenshot 3 at t=13785ms shows fully rendered story cards. The NetworkRequest evidence clearly shows /api/feed?feed=all with request@602ms and response@4751ms (4149ms TTFB), while /api/communities returned in ~666ms from the same time window, supporting the claim this is handler-specific latency rather than cold start. The response body contains HN-sourced stories (hn-48510010) with source:'mixed', and the absence of replay-request-id is confirmed in the response headers, justifying why no backend analysis was performed. All specific numbers cited in the root cause are traceable to evidence results. The root cause carefully stays within observable network data while noting the response composition.

Requirements: The screenshots clearly show the 'Loading stories...' placeholder state at t=1575ms (Images 1 and 2) and the fully rendered feed at t=13785ms (Image 3), confirming the user-visible impact. The /api/feed request takes 4151ms (602ms to 4751ms) with a TTFB of 4149ms, which exceeds the >3s critical threshold defined in the guidelines. The report thoroughly addresses the cold-start objection by showing three sibling Netlify functions issued at the same time (~598-602ms) all returned within ~700ms, proving the extra ~3.4s is specific to the feed handler's server-side processing, not infrastructure cold start. The root cause correctly identifies that the feed handler performs synchronous external content aggregation (Hacker News items visible in the response body). The report also properly notes the absence of a `replay-request-id` header, explaining why no backend recording analysis is possible, and characterizes the 24KB response payload contents. All specific numbers cited (timestamps, durations, request indices, story IDs) are traceable to the evidence results.

problems: root_cause: The response headers contain `x-nf-request-id: 01KTZ28YYSE87Q7K0B0EYE2HVZ`, which per guidelines should be submitted to the backend recorder service to create a backend recording for server-side analysis. The root cause currently speculates about 'slow server-side aggregation' based only on client-side response body inspection. Per guidelines, 'Reporting only client-side timing (TTFB, total duration) without server-side analysis is incomplete when a backend recording is available.' The root cause should include actual server-side profiling showing what the server spent its time on (e.g., database queries, external API calls to Hacker News, computation).
result.assessment: The screenshots show the 'Loading stories...' placeholder state at t=1575ms and the fully loaded feed at t=13785ms, confirming user-visible impact. The network evidence clearly shows /api/feed taking 4151ms (TTFB 4149ms) while sibling requests completed in ~700ms, which is strong evidence this isn't a platform-wide cold start. However, the report violates the guideline requiring server-side analysis when a backend recording is available. The response includes an `x-nf-request-id: 01KTZ28YYSE87Q7K0B0EYE2HVZ` header, which per the guidelines is a request ID that should be submitted to the backend recorder service to create a backend recording for server-side root cause analysis. The report speculates about 'slow server-side aggregation of mixed, externally-sourced content' based on the response body contents, but this is inference from the response payload, not actual server-side profiling. The guidelines explicitly state: 'Reporting only client-side timing (TTFB, total duration) without server-side analysis is incomplete when a backend recording is available.' Additionally, the response body is 24,468 chars (~24KB), and while the report mentions the payload contains mixed sources including HN content, it doesn't fully characterize the payload size or whether pagination is applied — though the body is under 100KB so this is less critical.

analysis.buggy_behavior: For roughly the first 4 seconds after the page loads, the main content area of the Digg homepage shows only 'Loading stories...' because the /api/feed?feed=all request that backs it does not respond until 4751ms (4151ms total).

submitted_data.description: GET /api/feed?feed=all returns 200 but only after 4151ms (TTFB 4149ms: request issued at 602ms, response received at 4751ms). It is the data source for the homepage story feed, so until it resolves the main content area renders a 'Loading stories...' placeholder. Three sibling API calls invoked at the same instant (/api/communities, /api/daily, /api/featured) all returned in ~700ms, so this is latency specific to the feed handler's own work, not a platform-wide cold start. The response body shows the handler aggregates 'mixed' sources including live Hacker News content (story ids like hn-48510010 and summaries 'A discussion-driven story from Hacker News'), pointing to slow server-side external fetching/aggregation.

evidence_result.assessment: Screenshots clearly show the 'Loading stories...' placeholder state (Images 1 and 2) and the fully rendered feed (Image 3), matching the chronology claims. All quantitative claims — 4149ms TTFB, request@602ms, response@4751ms, sibling /api/communities responding at 1264ms, image requests starting at 4884ms — are directly traceable to NetworkRequest evidence results. The root cause correctly identifies the /api/feed endpoint's 4149ms TTFB as the bottleneck, uses response body content (mixed source, HN story IDs) to support its claim about server-side aggregation, and rules out cold start by comparing with concurrent sibling endpoints. No replay-request-id header is present, so backend recording analysis is not required. The report is well-structured with concrete network metrics throughout.

problems: root_cause: The response headers contain `x-nf-request-id: 01KTZ28YYSE87Q7K0B0EYE2HVZ`, which per guidelines should be submitted to the backend recorder service to create a backend recording for server-side analysis. The root cause currently speculates about 'slow server-side aggregation' based only on client-side response body inspection. Per guidelines, 'Reporting only client-side timing (TTFB, total duration) without server-side analysis is incomplete when a backend recording is available.' The root cause should include actual server-side profiling showing what the server spent its time on (e.g., database queries, external API calls to Hacker News, computation).
requirements_result.assessment: The screenshots show the 'Loading stories...' placeholder state at t=1575ms and the fully loaded feed at t=13785ms, confirming user-visible impact. The network evidence clearly shows /api/feed taking 4151ms (TTFB 4149ms) while sibling requests completed in ~700ms, which is strong evidence this isn't a platform-wide cold start. However, the report violates the guideline requiring server-side analysis when a backend recording is available. The response includes an `x-nf-request-id: 01KTZ28YYSE87Q7K0B0EYE2HVZ` header, which per the guidelines is a request ID that should be submitted to the backend recorder service to create a backend recording for server-side root cause analysis. The report speculates about 'slow server-side aggregation of mixed, externally-sourced content' based on the response body contents, but this is inference from the response payload, not actual server-side profiling. The guidelines explicitly state: 'Reporting only client-side timing (TTFB, total duration) without server-side analysis is incomplete when a backend recording is available.' Additionally, the response body is 24,468 chars (~24KB), and while the report mentions the payload contains mixed sources including HN content, it doesn't fully characterize the payload size or whether pagination is applied — though the body is under 100KB so this is less critical.

result.assessment:
Evidence: Screenshot 1 and 2 show the Digg homepage with 'Loading stories...' placeholder text in the main content area at t=1575ms, confirming the loading state. Screenshot 3 at t=13785ms shows fully rendered story cards. The NetworkRequest evidence clearly shows /api/feed?feed=all with request@602ms and response@4751ms (4149ms TTFB), while /api/communities returned in ~666ms from the same time window, supporting the claim this is handler-specific latency rather than cold start. The response body contains HN-sourced stories (hn-48510010) with source:'mixed', and the absence of replay-request-id is confirmed in the response headers, justifying why no backend analysis was performed. All specific numbers cited in the root cause are traceable to evidence results. The root cause carefully stays within observable network data while noting the response composition.

Requirements: The screenshots clearly show the 'Loading stories...' placeholder state at t=1575ms (Images 1 and 2) and the fully rendered feed at t=13785ms (Image 3), confirming the user-visible impact. The /api/feed request takes 4151ms (602ms to 4751ms) with a TTFB of 4149ms, which exceeds the >3s critical threshold defined in the guidelines. The report thoroughly addresses the cold-start objection by showing three sibling Netlify functions issued at the same time (~598-602ms) all returned within ~700ms, proving the extra ~3.4s is specific to the feed handler's server-side processing, not infrastructure cold start. The root cause correctly identifies that the feed handler performs synchronous external content aggregation (Hacker News items visible in the response body). The report also properly notes the absence of a `replay-request-id` header, explaining why no backend recording analysis is possible, and characterizes the 24KB response payload contents. All specific numbers cited (timestamps, durations, request indices, story IDs) are traceable to the evidence results.

root_cause.text: The /api/feed?feed=all handler's own server-side processing takes ~4.1s before it sends any bytes (TTFB 4149ms: request@602ms -> response@4751ms), and this is application-level work, not a serverless cold start. Proof it is not a cold start: three sibling Netlify functions (/api/communities, /api/daily, /api/featured) were invoked in the SAME 598-602ms window and each responded in ~700ms, so any cold-start/runtime-init overhead is bounded at ~700ms and already accounted for; the feed endpoint's extra ~3.4s over its siblings is work specific to the feed handler. That extra work is synchronous aggregation of externally-sourced content performed at request time: the response is source:'mixed' and demonstrably contains live Hacker News items (story ids such as hn-48510010 and summaries reading 'A discussion-driven story from Hacker News') merged with the local seed stories, content the server must fetch from an external source on each request. The 24KB (brotli-compressed) JSON body is modest, so transfer time is negligible and the 4149ms is server processing/wait time, not payload size. No backend (server-side) Replay recording is available for deeper profiling: the response carries no `replay-request-id` header (the identifier the backend-recorder workflow consumes) -- only Netlify's own `x-nf-request-id: 01KTZ28YYSE87Q7K0B0EYE2HVZ`, which is not a Replay backend-recorder request id and cannot be submitted to the recorder service, so client-side timing plus the response-payload composition is the complete evidence obtainable here.

analysis.buggy_behavior: For roughly the first 4 seconds after the page loads, the main content area of the Digg homepage shows only 'Loading stories...' because the /api/feed?feed=all request that backs it does not respond until 4751ms (4151ms total).

submitted_data.description: GET /api/feed?feed=all returns 200 but only after 4151ms (TTFB 4149ms: request issued at 602ms, response received at 4751ms). It is the data source for the homepage story feed, so until it resolves the main content area renders a 'Loading stories...' placeholder. Three sibling API calls invoked at the same instant (/api/communities, /api/daily, /api/featured) all returned in ~700ms, so this is latency specific to the feed handler's own work, not a platform-wide cold start. The response body shows the handler aggregates 'mixed' sources including live Hacker News content (story ids like hn-48510010 and summaries 'A discussion-driven story from Hacker News'), pointing to slow server-side external fetching/aggregation.

evidence_result.assessment: Screenshot 1 and 2 show the Digg homepage with 'Loading stories...' placeholder text in the main content area at t=1575ms, confirming the loading state. Screenshot 3 at t=13785ms shows fully rendered story cards. The NetworkRequest evidence clearly shows /api/feed?feed=all with request@602ms and response@4751ms (4149ms TTFB), while /api/communities returned in ~666ms from the same time window, supporting the claim this is handler-specific latency rather than cold start. The response body contains HN-sourced stories (hn-48510010) with source:'mixed', and the absence of replay-request-id is confirmed in the response headers, justifying why no backend analysis was performed. All specific numbers cited in the root cause are traceable to evidence results. The root cause carefully stays within observable network data while noting the response composition.

requirements_result.assessment: The screenshots clearly show the 'Loading stories...' placeholder state at t=1575ms (Images 1 and 2) and the fully rendered feed at t=13785ms (Image 3), confirming the user-visible impact. The /api/feed request takes 4151ms (602ms to 4751ms) with a TTFB of 4149ms, which exceeds the >3s critical threshold defined in the guidelines. The report thoroughly addresses the cold-start objection by showing three sibling Netlify functions issued at the same time (~598-602ms) all returned within ~700ms, proving the extra ~3.4s is specific to the feed handler's server-side processing, not infrastructure cold start. The root cause correctly identifies that the feed handler performs synchronous external content aggregation (Hacker News items visible in the response body). The report also properly notes the absence of a `replay-request-id` header, explaining why no backend recording analysis is possible, and characterizes the 24KB response payload contents. All specific numbers cited (timestamps, durations, request indices, story IDs) are traceable to the evidence results.

duplicates.new_title: Story feed takes ~3-3.8s to load on every view (GET /api/feed) — content render is blocked behind a slow server response
duplicates.new_description: Every view of the story feed in the digg-clone is gated behind a GET /api/feed request that consistently takes 2984-3812ms to respond. This happens on the initial page load and on every subsequent feed switch (All, Trending, a community, search results), so the user repeatedly waits ~3+ seconds staring at a loading state before any stories appear. The latency is the same on warm calls late in the 313s session (3314ms at the 190s mark, 2984ms at the 300s mark), which rules out a serverless cold start. Sibling endpoints loaded at the same time are fast (/api/communities 106-415ms, /api/daily 106-415ms, /api/featured 212-415ms) and /api/feed?feed=saved returned in 113ms, so the slowness is specific to the feed-assembly code path on the server.
